questions.q: What kind of object is the famous "seamless celestial globe" of Mughal India?
options: A hollow metal sphere showing the stars and constellations
A glass bottle blown in one piece
A stone carving of the Earth
A wooden model of the Sun
questions.explanation: It is a hollow metal globe depicting the celestial sphere with stars and constellations engraved on its surface.

questions.q: Why are these globes called "seamless"?
options: They were carved from a single block of marble
They were cast as a single hollow piece with no joints or welds
They were riveted together so neatly the rivets disappear
They were painted to hide the gaps
questions.explanation: Each globe was cast hollow in one continuous piece, with no seams, soldering or welding joining separate halves.

questions.q: A celestial globe shows the positions of the stars as seen on which surface?
options: The imaginary sphere of the sky
questions.explanation: A celestial globe maps the apparent dome of the heavens, plotting stars and constellations on the imaginary celestial sphere.

questions.q: Cities such as Kashmir, Lahore and Sialkot, associated with these globes, lie in which broad region?
questions.explanation: Kashmir, Lahore and Sialkot are all centres in the Indian subcontinent (South Asia) where these globes were produced.

questions.q: During which Indian empire were the famous seamless celestial globes chiefly made?
questions.explanation: These globes were produced by metalworkers of the Mughal era, beginning in the late 16th century.

questions.q: The metalworking method used to create the seamless globes is a form of which casting technique?
questions.explanation: The globes were produced using the lost-wax (cire perdue) casting method to form a single hollow sphere.

questions.q: What metal alloy were the seamless celestial globes typically made of?
questions.explanation: The globes were cast from copper alloys such as bronze or brass, sometimes inlaid with silver.

questions.q: What did metalworkers and historians long believe about making a hollow metal globe in one seamless piece?
options: That it was cheap and easy
That only children could do it
questions.explanation: Casting a hollow sphere with no seam was long thought impossible, which is why these globes are celebrated as a metallurgical marvel.

questions.q: What are typically engraved across the surface of a seamless celestial globe?
options: Maps of trade routes on Earth
questions.explanation: The globes are engraved with stars, the figures of constellations, and reference circles such as the ecliptic and equator.

questions.q: Roughly how many such seamless celestial globes are known to survive in collections today?
questions.explanation: Only about twenty-odd seamless celestial globes are known to survive in museums and private collections worldwide.

questions.q: Which metalworker is credited with casting the first known seamless celestial globe, in 1589–90?
questions.explanation: Ali Kashmiri ibn Luqman cast the earliest known seamless celestial globe in AH 998 (1589–90 CE) at Lahore.

questions.q: Under which Mughal emperor's reign was the first seamless celestial globe produced?
questions.explanation: Ali Kashmiri made the first seamless globe in 1589–90, during the reign of the emperor Akbar.

questions.q: Which city in present-day Pakistan became the main centre producing these seamless globes over several generations?
questions.explanation: Lahore was the principal centre where a dynasty of metalworkers produced seamless celestial globes for generations.

questions.q: The constellation figures and star positions on the globes follow which earlier astronomical tradition?
options: The Greco-Islamic tradition descended from Ptolemy
questions.explanation: The globes follow the Greco-Islamic astronomical tradition, with constellations and star catalogues ultimately deriving from Ptolemy's Almagest.

questions.q: Star positions are commonly inlaid on the globes using which contrasting metal?
questions.explanation: The fixed stars were often marked with inlaid silver points so they would stand out against the bronze or brass surface.

questions.q: The number 1,018 sometimes associated with these globes refers to what?
options: The weight of a globe in kilograms
The year the technique was invented
The number of stars engraved, following the Ptolemaic catalogue
questions.explanation: Many globes plot the classical catalogue of 1,018 fixed stars inherited from the Ptolemaic / Greco-Islamic tradition.

questions.q: Why is the inside of a hollow casting so difficult to make seamless using the lost-wax method?
options: The wax is too heavy to melt
Metal cannot be melted in India
An internal clay core must be supported and then removed through tiny openings
questions.explanation: A hollow sphere needs an internal clay core held in place by pins; producing a thin, even shell with no seam and clearing the core was the great technical challenge.

questions.q: Modern scholarly study of these globes is closely associated with which historian of Islamic science?
questions.explanation: Historian Emilie Savage-Smith catalogued and analysed Islamicate celestial globes, documenting the seamless casting tradition.

questions.q: Besides being scientific instruments, the globes were also valued as what?
options: Prized works of art and status objects
questions.explanation: Beyond their astronomical function, the finely engraved globes were prized as luxury art objects and symbols of learning and status.

questions.q: A celestial globe differs from a terrestrial globe in that a celestial globe shows the heavens as if viewed from where?
options: From outside the celestial sphere looking in, so constellations appear mirror-reversed
From the bottom of a well
questions.explanation: A celestial globe presents the sky from an external (god's-eye) viewpoint, outside the celestial sphere, so the constellation figures appear mirror-reversed compared with the naked-eye view from Earth.

questions.q: Within which broader scientific instrument-making tradition do these seamless globes belong?
options: Indo-Islamic astronomical instrument making, alongside astrolabes
questions.explanation: The globes are part of the rich Indo-Islamic tradition of astronomical instruments, made alongside astrolabes and other devices.

questions.q: The inscriptions and labels on most of these globes are written in which language and script?
questions.explanation: Star names and inscriptions on the globes are typically engraved in Arabic and Persian, the scholarly languages of the Mughal court.

questions.q: The family workshop in Lahore that produced many seamless globes is often referred to in scholarship as which dynasty of instrument makers?
options: The Lahore family of astrolabists / globe makers
questions.explanation: A hereditary Lahore family of instrument makers, descended from the tradition of Ali Kashmiri, produced astrolabes and seamless globes across generations.

questions.q: Two great reference circles engraved on a celestial globe are the celestial equator and which other key circle?
options: The Tropic of Capricorn on Earth
questions.explanation: The ecliptic, the Sun's apparent yearly path through the zodiac, is engraved along with the celestial equator on these globes.

questions.q: Maker Diya' al-Din Muhammad of Lahore, who signed several surviving globes, worked mainly in which century?
questions.explanation: Diya' al-Din Muhammad of Lahore was a prolific 17th-century maker who signed numerous astrolabes and seamless celestial globes.

questions.q: Studies suggest the seamless globes were produced over roughly what span of time before the technique was lost?
options: About two centuries, into the 18th century
questions.explanation: The seamless-casting tradition flourished from the late 16th into the 18th century, after which knowledge of the technique faded.

questions.q: On a celestial globe the stars are grouped into the 48 classical constellations defined originally by whom?
questions.explanation: The 48 classical constellations engraved on the globes derive from Ptolemy's 2nd-century Almagest, transmitted through Islamic astronomy.

questions.q: Which feature of the casting most directly proves to modern scholars that a globe was made in one piece rather than joined?
options: The presence of two clear hemispheres bolted together
The complete absence of any seam line or solder joint on inner and outer surfaces
questions.explanation: Examination shows no seam, solder, or join on either surface, confirming the sphere was cast as a single hollow shell.

questions.q: Globes typically sat in a stand fitted with a graduated metal ring representing which structure of the sky?
options: The horizon and meridian rings of the celestial sphere
questions.explanation: A celestial globe was mounted in a frame with horizon and meridian rings, letting it model the sky for any latitude and time.

questions.q: What is the broader scientific significance attributed to these seamless globes today?
options: They prove the Earth is flat
They demonstrate advanced metallurgical and astronomical skill in Mughal-era South Asia
They were used to generate electricity
questions.explanation: The globes stand as evidence of sophisticated lost-wax metallurgy combined with astronomical learning in Mughal-era South Asia.
